prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

SCOPE OF WORK ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â‚¬Å“ UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA

COLLABORATOR:  GREGA REPOVS, PHD, INVESTIGATOR 
CORE(S):  INFORMATICS, DATA ANALYSIS, AND STATISTICS CORE C (IDASC)
PROJECT(S): N/A
SUBAWARD YEAR1 TOTAL:   $37,800 (DIRECTS+INDIRECTS)

SUMMARY STATEMENT:
THE INFORMATICS, DATA ANALYSIS, AND STATISTICS CORE (IDASC) WILL IMPLEMENT THE HUMAN CONNECTOME PROJECTÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S DATA ANALYSIS APPROACH FOR THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) PROJECTÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA. IT WILL ARCHIVE THE IMAGING DATA AS IT IS COLLECTED BY THE INTEGRATED DATA ACQUISITION CORE (IDAC) AND PROVIDE FEEDBACK ON DATA QUALITY CONCERNS. THE IDASC WILL ALSO CURATE AND MANAGE BIOLOGICAL, SOCIOLOGICAL, AND BEHAVIORAL MEASURES FROM THE GENETICS AND MULTI-OMICS SPECIMENS CORE (GMSC) AND IDAC. SUMMARY MEASURES WILL BE UPLOADED TO THE IDASC DATABASE FOR INTEGRATION AND PROJECT DISTRIBUTION.

THE IDASC WILL ALSO DEVELOP CROSS-SECTIONAL AND LONGITUDINAL DATA ANALYSIS PIPELINES TO GENERATE MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA PHENOTYPES (IDPS) REPRESENTING BRAIN ARCHITECTURAL/MORPHOMETRIC, FUNCTIONAL, AND CONNECTIVITY MEASURES AT THE LEVELS OF BRAIN AREAS, FUNCTIONAL NETWORKS, AND WHITE MATTER TRACTS. STATE OF THE ART TOOLS WILL BE PROVIDED BY IDASC TO (1) CONDUCT ASSOCIATION MODELLING ACCOUNTING FOR LONGITUDINAL STRUCTURE, (2) TEST DIFFERENT STRUCTURAL MODELS, PROBING SPECIFIC MEDIATING INFLUENCES ON ASSOCIATIONS, (3) USE REDUCED DIMENSION SUMMARIES FROM LARGE NUMBERS OF RELATED VARIABLES (4) IMPUTE MISSING DATA USING A UNIFIED APPROACH ACROSS THE AABC, AND (5) BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS TO (E.G.) USE IMAGING MARKERS TO PREDICT HEALTH OUTCOMES.  THE IDASC WILL ALSO BE RESPONSIBLE FOR DATA SHARING WITH THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

AIMS OF THE IDASC CORE:
AIM 1.	ARCHIVE IMAGING AND NON-IMAGING DATA, RUN IMAGE PREPROCESSING AND ANALYSIS PIPELINES, AND SHARE DATA AMONGST THE PROJECTS AND CORES.
AIM 2. DEVELOP AND MAINTAIN PREPROCESSING AND ANALYSIS PIPELINES, INCLUDING A LONGITUDINAL PIPELINE, TO PRODUCE MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA PHENOTYPES FOR ALL PROJECTS
AIM 3. PERFORM QUALITY CONTROL ON IMAGING DATA AND PROCESSING RESULTS. A
AIM 4. DEVELOP PROJECT STATISTICAL MODELS.

DR. REPOVS - RESPONSIBILITIES:
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SUPERVISE CONTAINERIZATION AND ACCEPTANCE TESTING OF PIPELINES DEVELOPED BY THE IDASC.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	DIRECT THE EFFORTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA TEAM (PIPELINE DEVELOPMENT) IDASC TEAM IN COLLABORATION WITH DR. ALAN ANTICEVIC TO ENSURE CORE AND PROJECT AIMS ARE MET. THE UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA QUNEX TEAM WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR:
O	MODIFYING THE (HCP) PIPELINES FOR AABC (STRUCTURAL, FUNCTIONAL, DIFFUSION, TASK FMRI ANALYSIS, MULTI-RUN ICAFIX, AND MSMALL) TO RUN IN THE QUNEX CONTAINER FOR THE CONNECTOME COORDINATION FACILITY (CCF) PROJECTS;
O	TESTING THE AABC PIPELINES WITH THE CONTAINER ON THE CENTER FOR HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTER (CHPC, SUPERCOMPUTER), AND DELIVERING A TESTED SINGULARITY VERSION OF THE QUNEX CONTAINER TO THE CCF PROCESSING TEAM THAT IS READY FOR BATCH PROCESSING; 
O	ENSURING THAT THE MODIFIED PIPELINES DELIVER COMPARABLE SCIENTIFIC RESULTS TO THOSE OF THE RECENT HUMAN CONNECTOME PROJECT AND LIFESPAN DATA RELEASES
O	PROVIDE BASH AND PYTHON CODE TO IMPLEMENT THE PROCESSING PIPELINES DEVELOPED BY THE IDASC CORE.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	PROVIDE EXPERTISE IN ALL ASPECTS OF THE QUNEX CONTAINERIZED PIPELINE SERVICE FOR THE IDASC.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH MEMBERS OF THE IDASC TO MODIFY HCP PIPELINES AS NEEDED TO ENSURE PIPELINE PERFORMANCE IS EFFICIENT, EFFECTIVE, AND SCALABLE FOR THE COMPUTING ENVIRONMENT.  MODIFICATIONS MAY INCLUDE:
O	UPGRADING AND UPDATING TOOLS, SUCH AS FREESURFER, FSL, MATLAB, AND EDDY; 
O	DESIGNING CHANGES AND CUSTOMIZATIONS TO ENSURE THE PIPELINES RUN SUCCESSFULLY IN THE QUNEX CONTAINER ON BOTH THE SUPERCOMPUTER AT WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE TODAY, AND TO PREPARE THEM TO RUN IN THE CONTAINER IN AN XNAT INTRADB INSTANCE IN THE NIMHÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S DATA ARCHIVE (NDA) INSTANCE OF THE AMAZON WEB SERVICES CLOUD BY THE END OF THE FUNDING PERIOD;
O	ENSURING THAT PIPELINE PERFORMANCE IS EFFICIENT, EFFECTIVE, AND SCALABLE FOR THE COMPUTING ENVIRONMENT.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SUBMIT INVOICES FOR PAYMENTS IN A TIMELY MANNER. (MONTHLY INVOICING RECOMMENDED.)
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SECURE DATA USE AGREEMENTS AS REQUIRED TO CONDUCT PROJECT ACTIVITIES.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH AABC CORES AND LEADERSHIP TO ENSURE THAT ALL DATA GENERATED BY THE CORES AND PROJECTS IS INCLUDED WITHIN SCHEDULED DATA FREEZES (MINIMUM ONE TIME PER YEAR) AND THAT THE DATA GENERATED IS MADE AVAILABLE TO THE EXTERNAL RESEARCH COMMUNITY.  

PERFORMANCE METRICS:
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	ATTEND AT LEAST 75% OF SCHEDULED AABC EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP COMMITTEE MEETINGS
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	PARTICIPATE IN IDASC AND WORKGROUP MEETINGS AS NEEDED TO MAINTAIN STUDY OPERATIONS, ENSURE TIMELY PROCESSING OF IMAGING DATA AND PIPELINE DEVELOPMENT AS NEEDED.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	MEET WITH AABC PERFORMANCE SITE TEAM MEMBERS, AND IDAC MEMBERS AS NEEDED TO ADDRESS DATA ACQUISITION AND PROCESSING QUALITY ISSUES.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	CONSULT WITH PROJECT LEADERS TO DEVELOP STATISTICAL MODELS AS NEEDED
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	WORK WITH AABC LEADERSHIP AND CORES TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT QUALITY ASSURANCE PLANS TO OPTIMIZE DATA COLLECTION AND PROCESSING.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SECURE AND MAINTAIN LOCAL IRB EXEMPT STATUS OR APPROVAL (IF REQUIRED) PRIOR TO ACCESSING STUDY DATA.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH AABC CORES AND LEADERSHIP TO ENSURE THAT ALL DATA GENERATED BY THE CORES AND PROJECTS IS INCLUDED WITHIN SCHEDULED DATA FREEZES (MINIMUM ONE TIME PER YEAR) AND THAT THE DATA GENERATED IS MADE AVAILABLE TO THE EXTERNAL RESEARCH COMMUNITY.  

ACTIVITIES TO SUPPORT THE GOALS OF THE AABC IDASC WILL BE MONITORED BY STUDY LEADERSHIP.  DIFFICULTY MEETING EXPECTATIONS IN ONE OR MORE AREAS WILL BE DISCUSSED WITH THE APPROPRIATE AABC LEADERSHIP COMMITTEE (E.G. SCIENTIFIC LEADERSHIP, EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP, MAIN PROTOCOL INVESTIGATORS, ETC.) TO DEVISE STRATEGIES TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE.  ADDITIONAL FOLLOW UP WITH CO-INVESTIGATORS INCLUDING TELECONFERENCE AND/OR AN IN-PERSON SITE VISIT MAY BE REQUIRED TO ADDRESS CONCERNS.  UNRESOLVED ISSUES WILL BE SHARED WITH THE AABC EXTERNAL ADVISORY COMMITTEE WHO WILL ISSUE A FINAL REPORT WITH RECOMMENDATIONS.  PROBATION AND/OR TERMINATION OF SUBAWARD AGREEMENTS MAY BE CONSIDERED IN THE EVENT OF NON-PERFORMANCE AND/OR UNRESOLVED ISSUES POTENTIALLY IMPACTING DATA COLLECTION, INTEGRITY, AND SCIENTIFIC GOALS INCLUDING SHARING OF DATA WITH THE EXTERNAL RESEARCH COMMUNITY.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

STATE OF THE ART TOOLS WILL BE PROVIDED BY IDASC TO (1) CONDUCT ASSOCIATION MODELLING ACCOUNTING FOR LONGITUDINAL STRUCTURE, (2) TEST DIFFERENT STRUCTURAL MODELS, PROBING SPECIFIC MEDIATING INFLUENCES ON ASSOCIATIONS, (3) USE REDUCED DIMENSION SUMMARIES FROM LARGE NUMBERS OF RELATED VARIABLES (4) IMPUTE MISSING DATA USING A UNIFIED APPROACH ACROSS THE AABC, AND (5) BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS TO (E.G.) USE IMAGING MARKERS TO PREDICT HEALTH OUTCOMES.  THE IDASC WILL ALSO BE RESPONSIBLE FOR DATA SHARING WITH THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

SCOPE OF WORK - YALE

COLLABORATOR:  ALAN ANTICEVIC, PHD, INVESTIGATOR 
CORE(S):  INFORMATICS, DATA ANALYSIS, AND STATISTICS CORE C (IDASC)
PROJECT(S): N/A
SUBAWARD YEAR1 TOTAL:   $25,125 (DIRECTS+INDIRECTS)
CUMULATIVE GRANT AWARD (LIFE OF GRANT):  

SUMMARY STATEMENT - IDASC:
THE INFORMATICS, DATA ANALYSIS, AND STATISTICS CORE (IDASC) WILL IMPLEMENT THE HUMAN CONNECTOME PROJECTÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S DATA ANALYSIS APPROACH FOR THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) PROJECTÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA. IT WILL ARCHIVE THE IMAGING DATA AS IT IS COLLECTED BY THE INTEGRATED DATA ACQUISITION CORE (IDAC) AND PROVIDE FEEDBACK ON DATA QUALITY CONCERNS. THE IDASC WILL ALSO CURATE AND MANAGE BIOLOGICAL, SOCIOLOGICAL, AND BEHAVIORAL MEASURES FROM THE GENETICS AND MULTI-OMICS SPECIMENS CORE (GMSC) AND IDAC. SUMMARY MEASURES WILL BE UPLOADED TO THE IDASC DATABASE FOR INTEGRATION AND PROJECT DISTRIBUTION.

THE IDASC WILL ALSO DEVELOP CROSS-SECTIONAL AND LONGITUDINAL DATA ANALYSIS PIPELINES TO GENERATE MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA PHENOTYPES (IDPS) REPRESENTING BRAIN ARCHITECTURAL/MORPHOMETRIC, FUNCTIONAL, AND CONNECTIVITY MEASURES AT THE LEVELS OF BRAIN AREAS, FUNCTIONAL NETWORKS, AND WHITE MATTER TRACTS. STATE OF THE ART TOOLS WILL BE PROVIDED BY IDASC TO (1) CONDUCT ASSOCIATION MODELLING ACCOUNTING FOR LONGITUDINAL STRUCTURE, (2) TEST DIFFERENT STRUCTURAL MODELS, PROBING SPECIFIC MEDIATING INFLUENCES ON ASSOCIATIONS, (3) USE REDUCED DIMENSION SUMMARIES FROM LARGE NUMBERS OF RELATED VARIABLES (4) IMPUTE MISSING DATA USING A UNIFIED APPROACH ACROSS THE AABC, AND (5) BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS TO (E.G.) USE IMAGING MARKERS TO PREDICT HEALTH OUTCOMES.  THE IDASC WILL ALSO BE RESPONSIBLE FOR DATA SHARING WITH THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

AIMS OF THE IDASC CORE:
AIM 1.	ARCHIVE IMAGING AND NON-IMAGING DATA, RUN IMAGE PREPROCESSING AND ANALYSIS PIPELINES, AND SHARE DATA AMONGST THE PROJECTS AND CORES.
AIM 2. DEVELOP AND MAINTAIN PREPROCESSING AND ANALYSIS PIPELINES, INCLUDING A LONGITUDINAL PIPELINE, TO PRODUCE MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA PHENOTYPES FOR ALL PROJECTS
AIM 3. PERFORM QUALITY CONTROL ON IMAGING DATA AND PROCESSING RESULTS. A
AIM 4. DEVELOP PROJECT STATISTICAL MODELS.

DR. ANTICEVIC - RESPONSIBILITIES:
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	DIRECT THE EFFORTS OF BOTH THE YALE (CONTAINER DEVELOPMENT) AND THE UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA TEAM (PIPELINE DEVELOPMENT) IDASC TEAM TO ENSURE CORE AND PROJECT AIMS ARE MET.
O	THE YALE TEAM WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR IMPLEMENTING THE IMAGE PROCESSING PIPELINES INTO DOCKER CONTAINERS SUITABLE FOR RUNNING ON THE WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY CENTER FOR HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTING (CHPC), BUILDING ON THE QUNEX LIBRARY. YALE COLLABORATORS WILL PROVIDE QUNEX CONTAINERS OPTIMIZED FOR EXECUTION ON CHPC
O	THE UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA QU|NEX TEAM WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR (1) MODIFYING THE (HCP) PIPELINES FOR AABC (STRUCTURAL, FUNCTIONAL, DIFFUSION, TASK FMRI ANALYSIS, MULTI-RUN ICAFIX, AND MSMALL) TO RUN IN THE QU|NEX CONTAINER FOR THE CONNECTOME COORDINATION FACILITY (CCF) PROJECTS (2) TESTING THE AABC PIPELINES WITH THE CONTAINER ON THE CENTER FOR HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTER (CHPC, SUPERCOMPUTER), AND DELIVERING A TESTED SINGULARITY VERSION OF THE QU|NEX CONTAINER TO THE CCF PROCESSING TEAM THAT IS READY FOR BATCH PROCESSING; (3) ENSURING THAT THE MODIFIED PIPELINES DELIVER COMPARABLE SCIENTIFIC RESULTS TO THOSE OF THE RECENT HUMAN CONNECTOME PROJECT AND LIFESPAN DATA RELEASES
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	PROVIDE EXPERTISE IN COMPUTATIONAL MODELING AND NEUROIMAGING ANALYSIS FOR THE IDASC.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	MODIFY HCP PIPELINES AS NEEDED TO ENSURE PIPELINE PERFORMANCE IS EFFICIENT, EFFECTIVE, AND SCALABLE FOR THE COMPUTING ENVIRONMENT.  MODIFICATIONS MAY INCLUDE:
O	UPGRADING AND UPDATING TOOLS, SUCH AS FREESURFER, FSL, MATLAB, AND EDDY; 
O	IMPLEMENTING CHANGES REQUIRED BY THE CCF SCIENTISTS TO ACHIEVE THE AIMS OF AABC;
O	DESIGNING CHANGES AND CUSTOMIZATIONS TO ENSURE THE PIPELINES RUN SUCCESSFULLY IN THE QU|NEX CONTAINER ON BOTH THE SUPERCOMPUTER AT WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY SCHOOL OF MEDICINE TODAY, AND TO PREPARE THEM TO RUN IN THE CONTAINER IN AN XNAT INTRADB INSTANCE IN THE NIMHÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S DATA ARCHIVE (NDA) INSTANCE OF THE AMAZON WEB SERVICES CLOUD BY THE END OF THE FUNDING PERIOD;
O	ENSURING THAT PIPELINE PERFORMANCE IS EFFICIENT, EFFECTIVE, AND SCALABLE FOR THE COMPUTING ENVIRONMENT.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SUBMIT INVOICES FOR PAYMENTS IN A TIMELY MANNER. (MONTHLY INVOICING RECOMMENDED.)
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SECURE DATA USE AGREEMENTS AS REQUIRED TO CONDUCT PROJECT ACTIVITIES.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH OTHER CORE AND PROJECT INVESTIGATORS AND THE COMMITTEE ON PUBLICATION AND DATA SHARING (CPDS) FOR MANUSCRIPT PREPARATION AND PRESENTATION AT SCIENTIFIC MEETINGS FOR DISSEMINATION OF DATA.

PERFORMANCE METRICS:
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	ATTEND ~75% OF SCHEDULED AABC EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP COMMITTEE MEETINGS
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	PARTICIPATE IN IDASC AND WORKGROUP MEETINGS AS NEEDED TO MAINTAIN STUDY OPERATIONS, ENSURE TIMELY PROCESSING OF IMAGING DATA AND PIPELINE DEVELOPMENT AS NEEDED.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	MEET WITH AABC PERFORMANCE SITE TEAM MEMBERS, AND IDAC MEMBERS AS NEEDED TO ADDRESS DATA ACQUISITION AND PROCESSING QUALITY ISSUES.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	CONSULT WITH PROJECT LEADERS TO DEVELOP STATISTICAL MODELS AS NEEDED
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	WORK WITH AABC LEADERSHIP AND CORES TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT QUALITY ASSURANCE PLANS TO OPTIMIZE DATA COLLECTION AND PROCESSING.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SECURE AND MAINTAIN LOCAL IRB EXEMPT STATUS OR SIRB APPROVAL (IF REQUIRED) PRIOR TO ACCESSING STUDY DATA.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH AABC CORES AND LEADERSHIP TO ENSURE THAT ALL DATA GENERATED BY THE CORES AND PROJECTS IS INCLUDED WITHIN SCHEDULED DATA FREEZES (MINIMUM ONE TIME PER YEAR) AND THAT THE DATA GENERATED IS MADE AVAILABLE TO THE EXTERNAL RESEARCH COMMUNITY.  

ACTIVITIES TO SUPPORT THE GOALS OF THE AABC IDASC WILL BE MONITORED BY STUDY LEADERSHIP.  DIFFICULTY MEETING EXPECTATIONS IN ONE OR MORE AREAS WILL BE DISCUSSED WITH THE APPROPRIATE AABC LEADERSHIP COMMITTEE (E.G. SCIENTIFIC LEADERSHIP, EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP, MAIN PROTOCOL INVESTIGATORS, ETC.) TO DEVISE STRATEGIES TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE.  ADDITIONAL FOLLOW UP WITH CO-INVESTIGATORS INCLUDING TELECONFERENCE AND/OR AN IN-PERSON SITE VISIT MAY BE REQUIRED TO ADDRESS CONCERNS.  UNRESOLVED ISSUES WILL BE SHARED WITH THE AABC EXTERNAL ADVISORY COMMITTEE WHO WILL ISSUE A FINAL REPORT WITH RECOMMENDATIONS.  PROBATION AND/OR TERMINATION OF SUBAWARD AGREEMENTS MAY BE CONSIDERED IN THE EVENT OF NON-PERFORMANCE AND/OR UNRESOLVED ISSUES POTENTIALLY IMPACTING DATA COLLECTION, INTEGRITY, AND SCIENTIFIC GOALS INCLUDING SHARING OF DATA WITH THE EXTERNAL RESEARCH COMMUNITY OUTSIDE THE GUIDELINES SET BY THE COMMITTEE ON PUBLICATIONS AND DATA SHARING (CPDS).

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

THE INFORMATICS, DATA ANALYSIS, AND STATISTICS CORE (IDASC) WILL IMPLEMENT THE HUMAN CONNECTOME PROJECTÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S DATA ANALYSIS APPROACH FOR THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) PROJECTÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã¢Â€ŽÂ¢S MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA. IT WILL ARCHIVE THE IMAGING DATA AS IT IS COLLECTED BY THE INTEGRATED DATA ACQUISITION CORE (IDAC) AND PROVIDE FEEDBACK ON DATA QUALITY CONCERNS. THE IDASC WILL ALSO CURATE AND MANAGE BIOLOGICAL, SOCIOLOGICAL, AND BEHAVIORAL MEASURES FROM THE GENETICS AND MULTI-OMICS SPECIMENS CORE (GMSC) AND IDAC. SUMMARY MEASURES WILL BE UPLOADED TO THE IDASC DATABASE FOR INTEGRATION AND PROJECT DISTRIBUTION.

THE IDASC WILL ALSO DEVELOP CROSS-SECTIONAL AND LONGITUDINAL DATA ANALYSIS PIPELINES TO GENERATE MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA PHENOTYPES (IDPS) REPRESENTING BRAIN ARCHITECTURAL/MORPHOMETRIC, FUNCTIONAL, AND CONNECTIVITY MEASURES AT THE LEVELS OF BRAIN AREAS, FUNCTIONAL NETWORKS, AND WHITE MATTER TRACTS. STATE OF THE ART TOOLS WILL BE PROVIDED BY IDASC TO (1) CONDUCT ASSOCIATION MODELLING ACCOUNTING FOR LONGITUDINAL STRUCTURE, (2) TEST DIFFERENT STRUCTURAL MODELS, PROBING SPECIFIC MEDIATING INFLUENCES ON ASSOCIATIONS, (3) USE REDUCED DIMENSION SUMMARIES FROM LARGE NUMBERS OF RELATED VARIABLES (4) IMPUTE MISSING DATA USING A UNIFIED APPROACH ACROSS THE AABC, AND (5) BUILD PREDICTIVE MODELS TO (E.G.) USE IMAGING MARKERS TO PREDICT HEALTH OUTCOMES.  THE IDASC WILL ALSO BE RESPONSIBLE FOR DATA SHARING WITH THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

AIMS OF THE IDASC CORE:
AIM 1.	ARCHIVE IMAGING AND NON-IMAGING DATA, RUN IMAGE PREPROCESSING AND ANALYSIS PIPELINES, AND SHARE DATA AMONGST THE PROJECTS AND CORES.
AIM 2. DEVELOP AND MAINTAIN PREPROCESSING AND ANALYSIS PIPELINES, INCLUDING A LONGITUDINAL PIPELINE, TO PRODUCE MULTI-MODAL IMAGING DATA PHENOTYPES FOR ALL PROJECTS
AIM 3. PERFORM QUALITY CONTROL ON IMAGING DATA AND PROCESSING RESULTS. A
AIM 4. DEVELOP PROJECT STATISTICAL MODELS.

ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SUPERVISE CONTAINERIZATION AND ACCEPTANCE TESTING OF PIPELINES DEVELOPED BY THE IDASC.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	DIRECT THE EFFORTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA TEAM (PIPELINE DEVELOPMENT) IDASC TEAM IN COLLABORATION WITH DR. ALAN ANTICEVIC TO ENSURE CORE AND PROJECT AIMS ARE MET. THE UNIVERSITY OF LJUBLJANA QUNEX TEAM WILL BE RESPONSIBLE FOR:
O	MODIFYING THE (HCP) PIPELINES FOR AABC (STRUCTURAL, FUNCTIONAL, DIFFUSION, TASK FMRI ANALYSIS, MULTI-RUN ICAFIX, AND MSMALL) TO RUN IN THE QUNEX CONTAINER FOR THE CONNECTOME COORDINATION FACILITY (CCF) PROJECTS;
O	TESTING THE AABC PIPELINES WITH THE CONTAINER ON THE CENTER FOR HIGH PERFORMANCE COMPUTER (CHPC, SUPERCOMPUTER), AND DELIVERING A TESTED SINGULARITY VERSION OF THE QUNEX CONTAINER TO THE CCF PROCESSING TEAM THAT IS READY FOR BATCH PROCESSING; 
O	ENSURING THAT THE MODIFIED PIPELINES DELIVER COMPARABLE SCIENTIFIC RESULTS TO THOSE OF THE RECENT HUMAN CONNECTOME PROJECT AND LIFESPAN DATA RELEASES
O	PROVIDE BASH AND PYTHON CODE TO IMPLEMENT THE PROCESSING PIPELINES DEVELOPED BY THE IDASC CORE.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	PROVIDE EXPERTISE IN ALL ASPECTS OF THE QUNEX CONTAINERIZED PIPELINE SERVICE FOR THE IDASC.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH MEMBERS OF THE IDASC TO MODIFY HCP PIPELINES AS NEEDED TO ENSURE PIPELINE PERFORMANCE IS EFFICIENT, EFFECTIVE, AND SCALABLE FOR THE COMPUTING ENVIRONMENT.  MODIFICATIONS MAY INCLUDE:

O	ENSURING THAT PIPELINE PERFORMANCE IS EFFICIENT, EFFECTIVE, AND SCALABLE FOR THE COMPUTING ENVIRONMENT.

ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SECURE DATA USE AGREEMENTS AS REQUIRED TO CONDUCT PROJECT ACTIVITIES.

PERFORMANCE METRICS:
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	ATTEND AT LEAST 75% OF SCHEDULED AABC EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP COMMITTEE MEETINGS
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	PARTICIPATE IN IDASC AND WORKGROUP MEETINGS AS NEEDED TO MAINTAIN STUDY OPERATIONS, ENSURE TIMELY PROCESSING OF IMAGING DATA AND PIPELINE DEVELOPMENT AS NEEDED.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	MEET WITH AABC PERFORMANCE SITE TEAM MEMBERS, AND IDAC MEMBERS AS NEEDED TO ADDRESS DATA ACQUISITION AND PROCESSING QUALITY ISSUES.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	CONSULT WITH PROJECT LEADERS TO DEVELOP STATISTICAL MODELS AS NEEDED
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	WORK WITH AABC LEADERSHIP AND CORES TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT QUALITY ASSURANCE PLANS TO OPTIMIZE DATA COLLECTION AND PROCESSING.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	SECURE AND MAINTAIN LOCAL IRB EXEMPT STATUS OR APPROVAL (IF REQUIRED) PRIOR TO ACCESSING STUDY DATA.
ÃƑÂ¢Ã¢Â€ŠÂ¬Ã‚Â¢	COLLABORATE WITH AABC CORES AND LEADERSHIP TO ENSURE THAT ALL DATA GENERATED BY THE CORES AND PROJECTS IS INCLUDED WITHIN SCHEDULED DATA FREEZES (MINIMUM ONE TIME PER YEAR) AND THAT THE DATA GENERATED IS MADE AVAILABLE TO THE EXTERNAL RESEARCH COMMUNITY.

SCIENTIFIC LEADERSHIP, EXECUTIVE LEADERSHIP, MAIN PROTOCOL INVESTIGATORS, ETC.) TO DEVISE STRATEGIES TO ADDRESS THE ISSUE.  ADDITIONAL FOLLOW UP WITH CO-INVESTIGATORS INCLUDING TELECONFERENCE AND/OR AN IN-PERSON SITE VISIT MAY BE REQUIRED TO ADDRESS CONCERNS.  UNRESOLVED ISSUES WILL BE SHARED WITH THE AABC EXTERNAL ADVISORY COMMITTEE WHO WILL ISSUE A FINAL REPORT WITH RECOMMENDATIONS. prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: VULNERABILITY AND RESILIENCY IN THE AGING ADULT BRAIN CONNECTOME (AABC) - ABSTRACT FOR OVERVIEW  The Aging Adult Brain Connectome (AABC) leverages the existing infrastructure developed by the Human Connectome Project for Aging (HCP-A) by obtaining longitudinal follow-up data (neuroimaging, cognitive testing, and blood) using a standardized protocol from a well characterized cohort of over 1,000 healthy individuals to generate within-participant brain trajectories for up to 10 years. At initial recruitment, individuals enrolled in the HCP-A were generally physically and cognitively healthy but over time some will develop preclinical AD or early cognitive changes due to AD or ADRD. The AABC is comprised of four Projects: Project 1 examine the effects of stress and allostatic load, including inflammation, during the early adult period. Project 2 examines the effects of lifestyle behaviors on the trajectory of cognitive and brain changes during the mid adult period. Project 3 examine the effects of menopause transition/vasomotor symptoms during the mid adult period. Project 4 exam- ine the clinical and neural indicators of resiliency and resistance to AD and ADRD in the later decades of adult- hood. The AABC also consists of 4 Cores: The Administration Core (AC) will provide essential core and site leadership to carry out the scientific mission of the AABC. The diversity recruitment and retention unit (DRRU) will be located within this core and will ensure that the AABC continues to recruit and retain an adequate distri- bution of races that is currently seen in the US. The Integrated Data Acquisition Core (IDAC) provides expertise and personnel from each site to acquire high quality neuroimaging, deep phenotyping of non-imaging data, and biosamples from each site.The Informatics, Data Analysis, and Statistics Core (IDASC) will house project imag- ing data using the IntraDB database, will perform quality control of raw and analyzed data, will develop and run cross-sectional and longitudinal pipelines to produce multi-modal imaging data phenotypes for each project, will provide dimension-reduced summaries, will impute missing data; and will develop and run statistical models for each project. The IDASC will also be responsible for data sharing with the general public. The Genetics and Multi-omics Specimens Core (GMSC) will provide genetic information on participants evaluated through the AABC who have been characterized using a uniform protocol. Multi-omic data and AD biomarker data will be generated by the GMSC.

prime_award_base_transaction_description: Planning: Building a Digital Twin-Based Virtual Engineering Laboratory for Students with Disabilities -With support from the Improving Undergraduate STEM Education: Hispanic-Serving Institutions (HSI Program), this planning project aims to develop an immersive virtual engineering laboratory that caters to the needs of students with disabilities, providing them with a transformative learning experience within an inclusive framework. In STEM education, engineering focuses on design, problem-solving, and construction. Preparing students for engineering roles necessitates a balance between theoretical instruction and practical exploration. However, traditional engineering laboratories frequently overlook the specific needs of students with disabilities, limiting their active engagement and learning outcomes. This challenge is especially evident given the significant number of students with disabilities in the U.S., the distinct barriers they face in STEM education, and the notable presence of both Hispanic/Latinx students and those with disabilities at Florida International University (FIU). To address this challenge, the research team will collaborate with the Disability Resource Center, the Center for the Advancement of Teaching, and the Center for Diversity and Student Success at FIU to develop an immersive virtual engineering environment that caters to the needs of students with disabilities, providing them with a transformative learning experience within an inclusive framework. By incorporating digital twin models and extended reality (XR) technologies, the study aims to bridge the gap between current teaching pedagogy and student needs, ultimately enhancing educational accessibility and equity for students with disabilities. This project aims to overcome the common barriers faced by students with disabilities by creating an inclusive and immersive learning environment. This innovative fusion of technology holds the potential to significantly enhance the learning experience and outcomes of students with disabilities, enabling students with diverse backgrounds and learning needs to participate in their STEM education actively and effectively. This project strives to provide actionable insights and solutions tailored to the needs of students with disabilities, aiming to enrich their academic experiences and boost their success in STEM education. By conducting surveys, ethnographic research, and interviews, the research team will delve into the unique needs and characteristics of students with disabilities in their STEM education experience. The creation of an inclusive virtual engineering laboratory, utilizing digital twin models combined with XR technologies, will redefine traditional learning methods, opening up novel avenues for student engagement. This technology-driven approach promises to transform the landscape of STEM education, empowering students with diverse learning needs to engage themselves actively and effectively in their engineering courses. The end goal is to champion inclusivity, elevate educational opportunities, and ensure academic achievements for students with disabilities. The HSI Program aims to enhance undergraduate STEM education and build capacity at HSIs. Projects supported by the HSI Program will also generate new knowledge on how to achieve these aims. This award reflects NSF's statutory mission and has been deemed worthy of support through evaluation using the Foundation's intellectual merit and broader impacts review criteria.- Subawards are not planned for this award.

subawardee_business_types: NONPROFIT ORGANIZATION,EDUCATIONAL INSTITUTION,STATE CONTROLLED INSTITUTION OF HIGHER LEARNING
subaward_description:
THIS STUDY IS MOTIVATED BY THE PRESSING NEED TO OVERCOME THE OBSTACLES ENCOUNTERED BY INDIVIDUALS WITH DISABILITIES, PARTICULARLY IN STEM EDUCATION, WHICH ARE OFTEN DUE TO INSUFFICIENT ACCOMMODATIONS AND LIMITED INCLUSIVITY. DR. QIANWEN GUO FROM FLORIDA STATE UNIVERSITY (FSU) WILL ASSIST DR. PRIYANKA ALLURI FROM FLORIDA INTERNATIONAL UNIVERSITY (FIU) TO COMPLETE THE RESEARCH TASK 2. 

BY INCORPORATING DIGITAL TWIN MODELS AND XR TECHNOLOGIES, THE STUDY AIMS TO BRIDGE THE GAP AND ENHANCE THE EDUCATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES AND SUCCESS OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES. THE RESEARCH QUESTIONS EXPLORE THE CHARACTERISTICS AND PRACTICAL NEEDS OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES IN STEM, THE DEVELOPMENT OF AN INCLUSIVE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB, AND THE IMPACT OF IMPLEMENTING SUCH A LAB ON THE LEARNING OUTCOMES OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES. ULTIMATELY, THE GOAL IS TO CONTRIBUTE VALUABLE INSIGHTS AND PRACTICAL SOLUTIONS THAT FOSTER INCLUSIVITY, IMPROVE EDUCATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES, AND PROMOTE THE SUCCESS OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES IN ACADEMIC SETTINGS. THROUGH THIS RESEARCH, WE AIM TO CONTRIBUTE VALUABLE INSIGHTS AND DEVELOP PRACTICAL SOLUTIONS THAT ADDRESS THE NEEDS OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES, ULTIMATELY ENHANCING THEIR EDUCATIONAL OPPORTUNITIES AND FOSTERING THEIR SUCCESS IN ACADEMIC SETTINGS. TO ACHIEVE THIS, THE FOLLOWING RESEARCH TASKS WILL BE ADDRESSED: 

TASK 1: RESEARCH ADVISORY BOARD (RAB)   
THE RESEARCH ADVISORY BOARD (RAB) WILL PROVIDE ASSISTANCE IN REFINING OUR RESEARCH ACTIVITIES AND ENGAGING RELEVANT STAKEHOLDERS ON CAMPUS. FIU WILL LEAD TASK 1. 

TASK 1.1: ANALYZE EXISTING DATASETS TO UNDERSTAND THE NEEDS OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES  UNDER THIS SUBTASK, WE WILL ANALYZE EXISTING DATASETS TO GAIN A COMPREHENSIVE UNDERSTANDING OF THE NEEDS OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES. 

TASK 1.2: DEVELOP QUESTIONNAIRES TO GATHER INSIGHTS FROM STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES TO GATHER INSIGHTS, THE FOLLOWING QUESTIONNAIRE DEVELOPMENT STEPS WILL BE TAKEN: FIRST, THE RESEARCH TEAM WILL COLLABORATE WITH THE CENTER FOR THE ADVANCEMENT OF TEACHING, CENTER FOR DIVERSITY AND STUDENT SUCCESS, AND DISABILITY RESOURCE CENTER TO GAIN VALUABLE INSIGHTS INTO THE UNIQUE CHALLENGES AND EXPERIENCES OF STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES. 

TASK 2: DEVELOPMENT OF THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB 
THIS RESEARCH TASK AIMS TO DEVELOP A HIGH-FIDELITY VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB THAT REPLICATES THE CHARACTERISTICS OF A REAL-WORLD ENGINEERING LAB.  FSU WILL LEAD TASK 2. 

TASK 2.1 REPRESENTING REAL-WORLD ENVIRONMENTS IN THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB 
AS WE ENGAGE IN SURVEYS AND CONDUCT FOCUS GROUP INTERVIEWS WITH STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES (TASK 1), WE WILL INQUIRE ABOUT THEIR ACCEPTANCE AND PREFERENCES REGARDING DIFFERENT INTERACTION MODALITIES.  

TASK 2.2 FUSING REAL-WORLD DATA INTO THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB 
WE WILL INCORPORATE REAL-WORLD DATA INTO THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB TO ENHANCE ITS AUTHENTICITY. 

TASK 2.3 DEMOCRATIZING THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB THROUGH CLOUD PLATFORMS 
TO ACCOMMODATE THE INCREASING PREVALENCE OF ONLINE DISTANCE LEARNING AND OVERCOME THE BARRIERS FACED BY STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES IN ATTENDING PHYSICAL CLASSES OR ACCESSING SPECIFIC DEVICES SUCH AS HMDS  (AS MENTIONED IN TASK 2.1), THIS TASK WILL LEVERAGE CLOUD PLATFORMS (E.G., MICROSOFT AZURE AND AMAZON WEB SERVICES) TO DEPLOY THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB ONLINE. 

TASK 2.4 CONFIGURING LEARNING MODULES 
THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB WILL CONSIST OF THREE DISTINCT LEARNING MODULES: EXPLORATION, IMPLEMENTATION, AND REFLECTION. 

TASK 3 PROTOTYPING AND ASSESSMENT OF THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB 
WHILE FULL-SCALE DEVELOPMENT AND DEPLOYMENT OF THE VIRTUAL ENGINEERING LAB MAY NOT BE PLAUSIBLE DURING THE TIME FRAME OF THIS PROJECT, WE WILL CREATE PROTOTYPES FOR TWO UNDERGRADUATE-LEVEL COURSES IN CIVIL AND CONSTRUCTION ENGINEERING AND ASSESS HOW STUDENTS WITH DISABILITIES PERCEIVE THE DEVELOPED PROTOTYPE WITH REGARDS TO ITS QUALITY, APPROPRIATENESS, AND USABILITY. FIU WILL LEAD TASK 3.

AS PART OF CORE D, THE SANFORD BURNHAM PREBYS MEDICAL DISCOVERY INSTITUTE (SBP) WILL SET UP AND MAINTAIN AN INFRASTRUCTURE NECESSARY FOR THE TRACKING OF SAMPLES, DATA AND METADATA GENERATED BY THE PROGRAM. THE OPENSOURCE SYSTEM SAGE SYNAPSE WILL BE USED AS CENTRAL HUB OF THIS WEB-BASED DATA MANAGEMENT SYSTEM, AND DATA WILL BE STORED EITHER ON A SERVER HOSTED AT SBP OR USING AMAZON WEB SERVICES (AWS) CLOUD STORAGE. TO ENSURE COMPREHENSIVE DATA ACCESS TO ALL MEMBERS OF THE PROGRAM, THE CORE WILL USE THIS INFRASTRUCTURE TO COORDINATE AND FACILITATE THE TRANSFER OF DATA BETWEEN PROJECTS AND CORES. THE CORE WILL ALSO PROVIDE STANDARD OPERATING PROCEDURES (SOPS) FOR THE CAPTURE OF METADATA, AND ASSIST THE PROJECTS AND CORES WITH THE IMPLEMENTATION OF THESE SOPS. CONTINUOUS USER SUPPORT OF PROJECT MEMBERS WILL BE PROVIDED THROUGH A HELPDESK THAT OFFERS TRAINING AND ASSISTANCE TO PROGRAM MEMBERS.

A KEY ROLE OF SBP WITHIN CORE D WILL BE THE DISSEMINATION OF PROGRAM DATA AND METADATA TO PUBLIC DATA REPOSITORIES. SPECIFICALLY, DATA GENERATED BY THE PROGRAM WILL BE DEPOSITED IN ACCORDANCE WITH THE NIAID DATA SHARING AND RELEASE GUIDELINES IN PUBLICLY ACCESSIBLE DATA REPOSITORIES SUCH AS GEO, SRA, MASSIVE, OR THE GENERALIST REPOSITORIES FIGSHARE AND ZENODO. METADATA WILL BE DISSEMINATED ALONGSIDE ALL DATA AS REQUIRED BY THE RESPECTIVE REPOSITORY.

BEYOND DATA MANAGEMENT, SBP WILL CONTRIBUTE TO THE DEVELOPMENT AND EXPANSION OF CORONASCAPE, A PUBLICLY AVAILABLE, WEB-BASED RESOURCE TO SEARCH, INTEGRATE, AND ANALYZE PUBLIC SARV-COV-2 OMICS DATA.

SBP, AS PART OF CORE D, WILL ALSO COORDINATE THE DISTRIBUTION OF NOVEL REAGENTS, INCLUDING PLASMIDS, CELL LINES, AND VIRAL CLONES, TO THE SCIENTIFIC COMMUNITY. THIS INCLUDES MONITORING THE GENERATION OF REAGENTS, MAKING THEM AVAILABLE TO THE SCIENTIFIC COMMUNITY UPON REQUEST, AND DEPOSITING REAGENTS CONSIDERED TO BE IMPORTANT OR IN HIGH DEMAND WITH THE BIODEFENSE AND EMERGING INFECTIONS RESEARCH RESOURCES REPOSITORY (BEI) AS REQUIRED.

subaward_description:
DR. GAUBE WILL OVERSEE ALL ASPECTS OF THE PROJECT THAT USE OCEAN COLOR OBSERVATIONS AS WELL AS 
PARTICIPATE AND HELP LEAD THE EDDY-CENTRIC ANALYSIS OF ALONG-TRACK ALTIMETRY DATA.  DR. GAUBE WILL 
ALSO LEAD A PUBLICATION HIGHLIGHTING THE UTILITY OF OCEAN COLOR DATA TO BE USED AS A TRACER FIELD 
REPRESENTATION OF MESOSCALE AND SUBMESOSCALE VARIABILITY DISCUSSING THE CAPABILITY OF THE 
CONCURRENT ANALYSIS OF ALONG-TRACK ALTIMETRY WITH COLLOCATED OCEAN COLOR IMAGERY.

IN YEAR 1 OF THE PROJECT GAUBE WILL UPDATE THE OCEAN COLOR DATABASE TO INCLUDE MODIS AND VIIRS DATA 
AS GAUBE HAS PRIMARILY USED SEAWIFS CHLOROPHYLL-A CONCENTRATION ESTIMATES (CHL) IN HIS PAST WORK.  
THIS UPDATE WILL REQUIRE SUBSTANTIAL STORAGE AND INTERPOLATION OF THE 1KM MODIS AND VIIRS DATA TO 
AN EDDY CENTRIC COORDINATE SYSTEM.  TO THIS END, GAUBE WILL USE THE AMAZON WEB SERVICES EC2 
INSTANCES COUPLED TO A SERIES OF EBS AND S3 STORAGE SYSTEMS.
THIS WORK IS EXPECTED TO REQUIRE NEARLY 1.3944 MONTHS OF EFFORT IN THE FIRST YEAR OF THE PROJECT.  
IN ADDITION, GAUBE WILL ATTEND THE OSTST MEETING AND THE OCEAN SCIENCES MEETING TO PRESENT ONGOING 
WORK ON THE ANALYSIS OF EDDY-CENTRIC CHL ESTIMATES.

YEAR TWO WILL SEE GAUBE FOCUSING ON REFINING THE METHODS DESCRIBED IN THE TECHNICAL DETAILS SECTION 
OF THE PROPOSAL WITH THE GOAL OF DEVELOPING AN EFFICIENT METHOD BY WHICH IT EXTRACT RELEVANT CHL 
ESTIMATES TO INDIVIDUAL EDDIES, OR SERIES OF EDDIES, THAT ARE IDENTIFIED AS POSSIBLY REPRESENTING 
MULTIPLE COHERENT VORTICES IN THE ALONG-TRACK OBSERVATIONS YET ARE REPRESENTED AS ISOLATED VORTICES 
IN THE MAPPED ALTIMETRY PRODUCTS.  THIS ANALYSIS WILL ALLOW US TO VALIDATE THE ESTIMATES OF EDDY 
SPLITTING, MERGING, OR THE DIVERSE STRUCTURE OF MESOSCALE VORTICES AND REPRESENTS THE MAJORITY OF 
GAUBE'S EFFORT IN YEAR TWO.

DURING THE THIRD YEAR OF THE PROJECT GAUBE WILL LEAD A PUBLICATION (THE PUBLICATION COSTS ARE 
INCLUDED IN PI LILLY'S BUDGET) DETAILING THE RESULTS OF THE ANALYSIS CONDUCTED IN YEAR TWO, WHICH 
WILL TAKE THE MAJORITY OF THE 1.8052 MONTH REQUESTED FOR GAUBE IN YEAR THREE.  ADDITIONALLY, GAUBE 
WILL WORK CLOSELY WITH LILLY AND EARLY TO INTERPRET THE RESULTS OF THE ALONG-TRACK EDDY TRACKING 
AND KINEMATICS.

THE FOURTH AND FINAL YEAR OF THE PROJECT WILL SEE GAUBE WORKING WITH LILLY AND EARLY TO FINALIZE 
PUBLICATION AND SUPPORT THE EFFORT OF EARLY AND THE PROGRAMMER TO ENSURE THAT THE ALONG TRACK AND 
OCEAN COLOR DATA IS MADE AVAILABLE ON OCEANEDDIES.ORG.  GAUBE WILL ALSO TRAVEL TO THE OSTST MEETING 
AND AGU FALL MEETING TO PRESENT RESULTS AND FINDINGS AS WELL AS THE UPDATED
OCEANEDDIES.ORG TOOL.

subaward_description:
LINEAR MULTI-PATTERN REGRESSION IS WELL SUITED TO CLIMATE TREND ANALYSIS AS APPLIED TO HIGHLY
ACCURATE MODERN SATELLITE DATA TYPES BECAUSE OF ITS COMPUTATIONAL EFFICIENCY AND EASE OF ERROR
ANALYSIS. IN THIS APPROACH, CLIMATOLOGIES OF MULTIPLE WEEKS OF SOUNDINGS ARE FIRST FORMED BASED
ON THE OBSERVABLE AND A LINEAR REGRESSION IS PERFORMED ON THE CLIMATOLOGIES TO OBTAIN ANOMALIES
AND TRENDS IN ATMOSPHERIC VARIABLES. IN SPECTRAL INFRARED SOUNDING, MANY THOUSANDS OF FOOTPRINTS
ARE AVERAGED IN LONGITUDE-LATITUDE BINS TO FORM AN AVERAGE SPECTRUM, AND THEN A LINEAR REGRESSION
USING OBSERVATION KERNELS AND UNCERTAINTY COVARIANCE MATRICES IS PERFORMED ON THE AVERAGE
SPECTRUM. A MAJOR SHORTCOMING IS THAT THE RESULTING TIME SERIES OF ANOMALIES IN ATMOSPHERIC
QUANTITIES IS SUBJECT TO NULL-SPACE ERROR: THERE ARE PATTERNS IN THE VERTICAL STRUCTURE OF THE RETRIEVED
ATMOSPHERIC VARIABLES THAT CANNOT BE SEEN IN THE OBSERVATIONS. THEY ARE THE COMPONENTS IN THE
VARIABLE SPACE OF SINGULAR-VALUE-DECOMPOSITION (SVD) ANALYSIS THAT HAVE ZERO EIGENVALUE. IN
INFRARED (IR) SOUNDING, THEY ARE SINUSOID WITH A VERTICAL THICKNESS THAT IS APPROXIMATELY THE WIDTH
OF THE CHANNEL WEIGHTING FUNCTIONS. THEY ARE READILY APPARENT IN AIRS LEVEL 3 PRODUCTS AND CAN
BE AS LARGE AS 3 K IN RETRIEVED TEMPERATURE.
IT IS POSSIBLE TO REMOVE THE IMPACTS OF NULL SPACE ERROR IN A CLIMATOLOGY BY COMBINING THE
ANOMALIES AND TRENDS OF ATMOSPHERIC VARIABLES AS INFERRED FROM TWO WHOLLY INDEPENDENT REMOTE
SENSING TECHNIQUES. EACH REMOTE SENSING TECHNIQUE, BY VIRTUE OF THEIR DIFFERENT PHYSICS, WILL INCUR
NULL SPACE ERRORS WITH DIFFERENT VERTICAL PATTERNS, ENABLING ANOTHER REGRESSION TO DETERMINE THE
NULL SPACE ERROR IN THE CLIMATOLOGIES OF EACH REMOTE SENSING TECHNIQUE AND REMOVE THEM. RADIO
OCCULTATION (RO) USING THE SATELLITES OF THE GLOBAL POSITIONING SYSTEM (GPS) CAN RETRIEVE
TEMPERATURE IN THE STRATOSPHERE, JUST AS SPECTRAL INFRARED NADIR SOUNDING DOES, BUT THE ASSOCIATED
NULL SPACE ERROR STRUCTURE IS AN EXPONENTIAL ENVELOP IN THE VERTICAL, IN REALITY SIMPLY PROPORTIONAL
TO INVERSE PRESSURE. STRATOSPHERIC TEMPERATURE AS RETRIEVED BY RO CAN BE SUBTRACTED FROM
STRATOSPHERIC TEMPERATURE AS INFERRED FROM SPECTRAL INFRARED SOUNDING LEAVING A RESIDUAL THAT IS A
COMBINATION OF NULL SPACE ERRORS. ONCE THE DIFFERENCE IS EXPLAINED AS A LINEAR COMBINATION OF THE
SINUSOIDAL NULL-SPACE ERROR OF IR SOUNDING AND THE INVERSE-PRESSURE NULL-SPACE ERROR OF GPS RO,
THE AMOUNT OF EACH CAN BE SUBTRACTED FROM THEIR RESPECTIVE CLIMATOLOGIES. IF IT WORKS PERFECTLY
AND NO OTHER ERROR IS PRESENT, THEN THE IR AND RO CLIMATOLOGIES SHOULD AGREE.
